name: SVT - AVNRT (Orthodromic)
identifyBy: P waves hidden within or immediately after QRS (short RP tachycardia)
Retrograde P waves - inverted in II, III, aVF if visible
Pseudo-R' in V1 or pseudo-S in II/III/aVF (retrograde P distorting the terminal QRS)

measurements.pWaves: Retrograde - hidden in QRS or immediately after

management: Vagal manoeuvres first: Valsalva (modified - legs elevated), carotid sinus massage
Adenosine 6mg rapid IV push with fast flush - if vagal fails (warn: brief asystole, chest discomfort)
Verapamil 5mg IV if adenosine fails or unavailable (avoid in WPW, hypotension)
pearls: AVNRT is the most common form of SVT - re-entry circuit within the AV node
Modified Valsalva: strain for 15s → supine → legs elevated 45° for 15s - doubles conversion rate
Adenosine has a half-life of <10 seconds - must be given as rapid bolus with fast saline flush
Pseudo-R' in V1 is the most reliable ECG sign of AVNRT - caused by retrograde P wave distorting terminal QRS
Recurrent SVT: referral for EP study and ablation (>95% cure rate)
differentials: Sinus tachycardia (gradual onset, P before QRS)
Atrial tachycardia (P wave before QRS, different morphology)

qaNotes: Regular narrow-complex SVT; P waves hidden or retrograde.
